1.name: Premature Optimization Before Measurement
1.what_they_do: Add caching, threading, or async I/O before identifying the actual bottleneck
1.why_it_seems_right: Performance best practices say these are good things
1.when_it_kills_you: You optimize the wrong resource. CPU optimization when the bottleneck is disk I/O. Async I/O when the bottleneck is computation.
1.what_to_do_instead: Measure first. Identify which resource saturates. Then optimize THAT resource.
1.signal_you_fell_for_it: You can't explain which specific resource limit your optimization addresses

2.name: Local Memory as 'Temporary' Cache
2.what_they_do: Store session/state in process memory 'just for now' with plans to externalize later
2.why_it_seems_right: It's simpler. Works in dev. Plans to fix it later.
2.when_it_kills_you: Second instance deployed. Users randomly lose state. 'Later' never comes before production.
2.what_to_do_instead: External state from day one. The extra 2 hours of setup saves 2 weeks of debugging.
2.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your service works perfectly with 1 instance but breaks with 2

2.name: Session Affinity as Stateless
2.what_they_do: Use sticky sessions to route user always to same instance, call it 'stateless'
2.why_it_seems_right: State stays on one machine per user. Looks scalable.
2.when_it_kills_you: Instance dies → all sticky users lose state. Uneven load (one instance gets all heavy users). Can't auto-scale down.
2.what_to_do_instead: True statelessness: any instance can serve any request. State is external.
2.signal_you_fell_for_it: You need to drain connections during deploy and some users still lose data

3.name: Unbounded Write Buffer
3.what_they_do: Buffer all writes in memory, flush periodically or on shutdown
3.why_it_seems_right: Batching improves throughput. Fewer disk operations.
3.when_it_kills_you: Process crash (kill -9, OOM, power loss) before flush — all buffered writes lost. Data acknowledged to client is gone.
3.what_to_do_instead: Write-ahead log with configurable flush interval. Accept the throughput cost for durability.
3.signal_you_fell_for_it: Restarting your service loses recently written data

3.name: Index Everything
3.what_they_do: Add indexes on every column/field 'for performance'
3.why_it_seems_right: Reads are faster with indexes. More indexes = more fast.
3.when_it_kills_you: Write throughput collapses. Each write updates N indexes. Write amplification causes disk I/O saturation.
3.what_to_do_instead: Index only access patterns that are actually slow. Measure read vs write ratio. Each index is a trade-off, not a free optimization.
3.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your writes are slower than your reads, or adding data takes longer than reading it

4.name: Cache Everything Forever
4.what_they_do: No TTL, no invalidation. Cache grows until memory pressure.
4.why_it_seems_right: Never pay the database cost twice. Maximum performance.
4.when_it_kills_you: Source data changes. Cache serves stale data indefinitely. Users see inconsistent state. Business logic makes decisions on old data.
4.what_to_do_instead: Every cached item needs an invalidation strategy. TTL as a safety net even with event-driven invalidation.
4.signal_you_fell_for_it: Users report 'changes not saving' but the database has the correct value

4.name: Cache In Front of Every Read
4.what_they_do: Indiscriminate caching: cache database results, API responses, computed values — all with same strategy
4.why_it_seems_right: Less database load is always good. Latency goes down.
4.when_it_kills_you: Cache churn on rarely-accessed keys wastes memory. Cache invalidation complexity explodes. Debugging requires checking 3 layers.
4.what_to_do_instead: Cache only hot data (Pareto: 20% of keys serve 80% of requests). Different TTLs for different access patterns.
4.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your cache hit ratio is below 50% — you're paying the complexity cost without the benefit

5.name: Fire and Forget
5.what_they_do: Publish message to queue and assume it will be processed. No tracking, no dead-letter, no monitoring.
5.why_it_seems_right: Async is async. The consumer will handle it eventually.
5.when_it_kills_you: Consumer crashes. Message lost. No record of failure. Silent data loss that surfaces days later as missing records.
5.what_to_do_instead: At-least-once delivery with consumer acknowledgment. Dead-letter queue for failures. Monitor queue depth and consumer lag.
5.signal_you_fell_for_it: You discover missing data days after the event and can't determine when or why it was lost

5.name: Infinite Retries Without Dead-Letter
5.what_they_do: Retry failed messages forever. Never give up.
5.why_it_seems_right: The message must be processed. Persistence is a virtue.
5.when_it_kills_you: Poison message blocks the queue. All subsequent messages are delayed. Processing stops for valid messages.
5.what_to_do_instead: Max retry count + dead-letter queue. Alert on dead-letter growth. Process dead letters manually or with different logic.
5.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your queue depth is growing but consumer is busy (processing the same message over and over)

6.name: Read From Leader Always
6.what_they_do: All reads go to leader 'for consistency.' Replicas exist only for failover.
6.why_it_seems_right: Strong consistency. No stale data. Simple mental model.
6.when_it_kills_you: Leader becomes bottleneck. Replicas are wasted capacity. Single point of failure for both reads and writes.
6.what_to_do_instead: Identify which reads NEED strong consistency (very few). Route those to leader. Route the rest to replicas.
6.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your leader is at 90% capacity while replicas are at 5%, and you're considering scaling the leader vertically

6.name: Synchronous Replication Everywhere
6.what_they_do: All writes wait for all replicas to acknowledge before returning success
6.why_it_seems_right: No data loss. Strong consistency. Peace of mind.
6.when_it_kills_you: One slow replica makes ALL writes slow. Replica failure makes writes unavailable. You've traded availability for consistency everywhere, even where you don't need it.
6.what_to_do_instead: Synchronous to ONE replica (minimum durability guarantee). Async to others. Accept the trade-off explicitly.
6.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your write latency is determined by your slowest replica, not your leader

7.name: Shard by Entity Without Considering Access Patterns
7.what_they_do: Hash partition by primary key. Done.
7.why_it_seems_right: Even distribution. Simple routing. Textbook answer.
7.when_it_kills_you: Cross-partition queries (list by date, search by attribute, aggregations) become scatter-gather nightmares. One query hits ALL shards.
7.what_to_do_instead: Shard by the most common ACCESS PATTERN, not by entity identity. Accept that some queries become expensive and plan for them (denormalization, secondary indexes, CQRS).
7.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your most common list/search queries fan out to every shard and are getting slower as you add data

8.name: Distributed Lock Without Fencing
8.what_they_do: Acquire lock. Do work. Release lock. Assume only one holder at a time.
8.why_it_seems_right: That's what locks do. Mutual exclusion. Simple.
8.when_it_kills_you: Lock expires during GC pause or network delay. Second process acquires lock. First process resumes and writes without checking. Both think they have exclusive access.
8.what_to_do_instead: Fencing tokens: lock includes a monotonically increasing token. The protected resource rejects writes with tokens older than the most recent.
8.signal_you_fell_for_it: You see impossible data states that 'couldn't happen if the lock was working correctly'

9.name: Distributed Monolith
9.what_they_do: Split into services but require synchronous calls between all of them for every request
9.why_it_seems_right: Services are supposed to be independent. We split them.
9.when_it_kills_you: Availability is PRODUCT of all service availabilities: 99.9% × 99.9% × 99.9% = 99.7%. One slow service makes everything slow. One down service makes everything down.
9.what_to_do_instead: Async communication where possible. Circuit breakers on sync paths. Each service must degrade gracefully when dependencies are unavailable.
9.signal_you_fell_for_it: Deploying one service requires coordinating deploys of three others, or one service's outage takes down the whole system

10.name: Alerting on Causes Instead of Symptoms
10.why_it_seems_right: Resource exhaustion causes outages. Catch it early.
10.when_it_kills_you: CPU at 85% might be fine (batch processing). CPU at 40% might be bad (because it was 5% yesterday). Alert fatigue: hundreds of alerts, most meaningless.
10.what_to_do_instead: Alert on user-facing symptoms: error rate > threshold, latency > SLO, throughput drop. Use resource metrics for DIAGNOSIS, not alerting.
10.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your on-call engineer ignores 80% of alerts because they're not actionable

11.name: Retry Without Backoff or Jitter
11.what_they_do: Immediate retry on failure. If it fails, retry again. Fixed interval.
11.why_it_seems_right: The request might work next time. Persistence.
11.when_it_kills_you: Recovering service gets hit by a wall of retries. N clients × R retries = N×R requests on recovery. Thundering herd causes second outage.
11.what_to_do_instead: Exponential backoff with jitter. Clients spread their retries over time. Recovering service gets a gradual ramp, not a wall.
11.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your service recovers from an outage, gets a traffic spike 3x normal, and fails again

11.name: Circuit Breaker With Wrong Timeout
11.what_they_do: Circuit breaker open duration set to arbitrary value (30s, 60s) without understanding the dependency's recovery time
11.why_it_seems_right: Any circuit breaker is better than none. Just pick a number.
11.when_it_kills_you: Too short: circuit oscillates between open/closed during partial recovery. Too long: dependency recovered 5 seconds ago but you're still refusing requests for 55 more seconds.
11.what_to_do_instead: Measure dependency's actual recovery time. Set half-open probe interval shorter than full open duration. Use canary requests to test recovery.
11.signal_you_fell_for_it: Your dashboard shows the dependency recovered but your service is still returning errors

12.name: Build for Projected Maximum
12.what_they_do: Product says 100x growth in 2 years. Engineer builds for 100x immediately.
12.why_it_seems_right: Be prepared. Don't want to rebuild. Future-proof.
12.when_it_kills_you: Cost: 100x infrastructure for 1x traffic. Complexity: operating a distributed system you don't need yet. The projection was wrong: growth was 5x, not 100x.
12.what_to_do_instead: Build for current + 3-5x headroom. PLAN for 10x (migration path documented). Build 10x only when you're at 3x and can validate the projection.
12.signal_you_fell_for_it: You're running (and paying for) a 50-node Kafka cluster for 100 messages per second
